Aerospace Mar 29, 2026

Satellite imaging industry’s next challenge: getting systems to talk to each other

Executives say lack of incentives and interoperability slows ‘tipping and cueing’ across vendors...

Satellite imagery providers must overcome vendor fragmentation to enable real-time intelligence sharing, a critical capability for defense and commercial aviation operations.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Why Do Airlines Turn Off The Cabin Lights During Takeoff And Landing?

This practice ensures that passengers are better prepared during an emergency.

Darkened cabins enable passenger eye adaptation to external lighting conditions, allowing faster evacuation decision-making if emergency slides are deployed during critical phases.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Here’s How US Airline Economy Seat Pitch Compares In 2026

Even an extra inch of seat pitch can make all the difference to passenger comfort.

Most US carriers have stabilized economy seat pitch around 31-32 inches for 2026, suggesting the race to minimize legroom may have reached practical limits.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

6 Commercial Aircraft With The Largest Wingspans Flying Today

The biggest wings in the sky in 2026.

Modern ultra-wide-body freighters and passenger aircraft now prioritize fuel efficiency and payload capacity through extended wingspans rather than fuselage length, reshaping cargo and long-range operations.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation from investigating accidents after they occur to systematically identifying and mitigating hazards before incidents happen.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Chaos Continues: TSA Callouts Reach Record Numbers As Over 500 Officers Quit

The recent departure of over 500 TSA officers has left security checkpoints nationwide grappling with worsening delays.

Accelerating TSA workforce attrition directly threatens checkpoint throughput capacity, forcing airlines to absorb operational ripple effects through schedule compression and potential slot reductions.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Why Many Airlines Choose Either Airbus Or Boeing

Both manufacturers have strengths, but there are benefits to standardization.

Fleet standardization on a single manufacturer reduces pilot training costs, maintenance complexity, and spare parts inventory despite losing competitive leverage with suppliers.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Southwest Airlines Captain Flies Retirement Flight With First Officer Son

A Southwest captain’s final flight with his son highlights aviation family legacy, echoed by a similar emotional father-daughter story in Turkey.

Multi-generational pilot families demonstrate sustained aviation career appeal despite industry recruitment challenges and economic pressures on pilot compensation.

See Simple Flying →

Air Cargo Mar 29, 2026

Here’s How Much More Cargo The Boeing 747-8F Carries Compared To The 777F

The 747-8F and 777F represent the pinnacle of what Boeing currently offers to the cargo market. Find out what sets these two giants apart.

The 747-8F's larger fuselage provides operators 58% more cargo volume than the 777F, though higher fuel consumption may limit profitability on longer regional routes.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Organizations must document their rationale for accepting residual risks that remain after implementing feasible controls, creating an audit trail that demonstrates safety management maturity beyond compliance checkboxes.

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

How The Queen Of The Skies Made A Comeback At Delta Air Lines

The story behind the 747's last hurrah in the US.

Delta's 747 retirement signals the end of an era for wide-body quad-jet operations in US domestic networks, as airlines consolidate on more fuel-efficient twin-engine alternatives.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 29, 2026

Misawa’s 13th Fighter Squadron Receives First F-35s

Misawa Air Base has received the U.S. Air Force’s first F-35A Lightning II fighters to be permanently based in Japan, beginning the transition from the F-16 to the fifth-gen aircraft. Misawa Air Base, home of the U.S. Air Force’s 35th Fighter Wing, has received its first four F-35A Lightning II...

Japan's first permanent F-35 basing marks a strategic shift in U.S. Indo-Pacific air superiority capabilities against emerging peer competitors.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Why Do US Airlines Still Use The Boeing 757 On Key Routes?

Dozens of 757s still fly to this day, but four decades after the aircraft's introduction, why is this?

The 757's unmatched range-to-capacity ratio and proven economics on thin transatlantic routes make it irreplaceable until widebody narrowbodies achieve similar operational efficiency.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 29, 2026

Tight Space: Inside The B-2 Spirit Cockpit

The B-2 Spirit has to perform extra-long missions, while providing less space and comfort for its pilots than transport aircraft like the C-17.

B-2 pilots endure extreme operational constraints during 20+ hour missions in a cockpit designed for stealth over ergonomics, highlighting the design trade-offs inherent in strategic bomber development.

See Simple Flying →

KSQL Noise Abatement Departure

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo,...

San Carlos Airport's noise-abatement procedures reflect operational complexity within overlapping airspace constraints that require coordinated departure management in congested metropolitan regions.

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Press Release: Aer Lingus takes off with Starlink inflight Wi-Fi

Aer Lingus has equipped the first aircraft in its fleet with Starlink, delivering ultrafast, reliable Wi-Fi to customers and marking a major step forward for customer experience. Customers travelling from Dublin to New York JFK on flight EI105 today are the first to experience the innovative...

Aer Lingus's Starlink deployment signals satellite-based connectivity is moving from niche offering to mainstream fleet integration, potentially pressuring traditional air-to-ground Wi-Fi providers across European carriers.

See Runway Girl Network →

Unidentified Drones Crash Near Kouvola, Finland, Authorities Investigate Possible Origin

Finnish authorities are investigating reports that one or two unmanned aerial systems havecrashed in southeastern...

Nordic airspace incursions by unidentified drones signal escalating security gaps in European border monitoring requiring urgent coordination with NATO allies.

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Op-Ed: Seating architecture must evolve to accommodate power, IFEC

Please enjoy this Op-Ed contribution from Unum VP Business Development, Alan McInnes Moving beyond the traditional considerations of structure, comfort and certification, aircraft seating is a critical integration point for power, in-flight entertainment (IFE) and connectivity systems that...

Integrated power delivery through seat architecture is becoming essential infrastructure rather than optional amenity, driving seating redesign across certification and manufacturing processes.

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

India inaugurates Noida International Airport, Delhi’s second gateway

India opened the first phase of Noida International Airport (DXN) in Jewar, Uttar Pradesh, on March 28, 2026,…...

India's second Delhi-area airport reduces congestion at Indira Gandhi International and positions the region for 72 million annual passengers, reshaping South Asian aviation connectivity.

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Helicopter pilots systematically underestimate wire strike danger due to closure speeds exceeding 100 mph, making avoidance impossible even with visual detection.

USAF E-3 Sentry AWACS damaged in Iranian strike on Saudi air base

An Iranian missile and drone attack on Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia on March 27, 2026,…...

The E-3 Sentry's vulnerability to coordinated missile-drone attacks signals urgent need for AWACS fleet modernization and enhanced air defense integration across allied Middle Eastern bases.

Aerospace Mar 29, 2026

‘Shots on goal and win the game’: NASA’s effort to accelerate lunar landings

While NASA outlined plans to increase the cadence of robotic lunar lander missions at a recent event, it said little about accelerating work on crewed lunar landers.

NASA's focus on accelerating robotic lunar missions contrasts with its cautious approach to crewed lander development, signaling divergent timelines for achieving sustained lunar presence.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 29, 2026

USAF A-10 strike on Iraqi military base kills seven soldiers

An airstrike attributed to a US Air Force A-10 Thunderbolt II hit a military clinic at the Habbaniyah…...

Civilian casualties in precision strike operations underscore ongoing challenges in distinguishing military targets within shared base infrastructure during close air support missions.

Military Mar 28, 2026

Northrop Grumman Testbed With AIM-260-Like Nose Spotted Flying Alongside Raytheon’s VOODOO 1 at Eglin AFB

The two testbeds, known by their callsigns SCAN 06 and VOODOO 1, carried out concurrent operations in Eglin’s highly instrumented airspace. For three consecutive days, on Mar. 24, 25 and 26, two testbed aircraft, the Northrop Grumman CRJ-700 carrying the U.S. registration code N806X and typically...

Northrop Grumman and Raytheon are conducting joint air-to-air missile testing operations, suggesting accelerated development of next-generation air combat systems for U.S. fighters.

See The Aviationist →

Industry Mar 29, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all aviation levels offers a scalable pathway to accommodate projected traffic growth while simultaneously reducing environmental impact without requiring complete fleet replacement.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Starfish Space finds a new partner for docking demonstration mission

Starfish Space has changed plans for a docking demonstration mission after its original partner backed out.

Starfish Space's ability to secure alternative partnerships despite setbacks suggests growing confidence in their on-orbit servicing technology among multiple spacecraft operators.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Germany Considering MQ-28A Ghost Bat CCA, Says Defense Minister

German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius said the MQ-28 Ghost Bat is among the uncrewed aircraft being evaluated to support the Eurofighter Typhoon fighter fleet. Germany’s defense minister Boris Pistorius said during his visit to Australia on Mar. 27, 2026, that Berlin is considering Boeing Defence...

Germany's evaluation of the MQ-28 Ghost Bat signals NATO's strategic shift toward integrating autonomous crewed aircraft support with legacy fighter fleets to offset pilot workload and extend operational effectiveness.

See The Aviationist →

Military Mar 28, 2026

USMC F-35Cs and USS Tripoli ARG Arrive in the Middle East

A month after the start of the hostilities, the U.S. Marine Corps is increasing its presence in the Middle East with the arrival of a F-35C squadron and the USS Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group. Mar. 28, 2026, marks a month since the U.S. and Israel launched Operations Epic Fury and Roaring Lion,...

The USMC's deployment of F-35C carrier-variant fighters alongside an ARG signals escalated regional air-combat capability and sustained multi-domain operations commitment in Middle East tensions.

See The Aviationist →

Geopolitical volatility in the Middle East is directly impacting aviation fuel policy implementation, signaling airlines' exposure to oil price instability.

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

Artemis 2's arrival marks the final operational phase before humanity's return to lunar orbit, with launch readiness now dependent on remaining ground systems verification and weather conditions.

Military Mar 27, 2026

[Updated] Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s, E-3 Sentry

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Damage to KC-135 tankers directly threatens U.S. air operations' refueling capacity across the Middle East, potentially constraining extended-range missions for months during repairs.

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

The Near Space Network's mission-critical communication infrastructure, managed by veterans like Richards, represents decades of accumulated expertise now essential for NASA's lunar return missions.

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M contract with Intuitive Machines signals private industry's central role in Artemis lunar infrastructure, reducing government costs while accelerating payload delivery timelines.

See NASA →

NASA's environmental awards program establishes measurable criteria for aerospace organizations to demonstrate sustainability leadership beyond regulatory compliance.
